title: Fitting a cold water tank ball valve
subtitle: Replace a faulty inlet valve in a cold water storage cistern
excerpt: How to replace the ball valve in a cold water cistern: isolating the supply, removing the old valve body and float arm, fitting the new valve at the correct inlet size, adjusting the float arm for the right water level, and restoring supply.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. BS 1212 Float Operated Valves. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A float-operated inlet valve in a cold water cistern. As the water level rises, the float arm lifts and closes the valve; as the cistern empties, the arm falls and opens it to refill. The name comes from older designs that used a hollow copper ball as the float.

An open-topped cold water storage tank, usually located in the loft, that supplies cold water to the hot water cylinder and sometimes to cold taps by gravity. Distinct from a sealed pressurised vessel.

content.text: either fails to shut off (causing the overflow pipe to run continuously) or fails to open fully (causing slow refill). Both faults are fixed by fitting a new valve. Modern plastic-bodied ball valves are direct replacements for the older brass types and fit the same threaded inlet port.

A replacement ball valve in the correct inlet size: 15 mm for most domestic cold water cisterns. A compatible float arm (usually supplied with the valve). PTFE tape. Adjustable spanner and water pump pliers. Towels. The cistern is normally in the loft; take a torch and a stable platform to work from.
Step 1, isolate and drain the cistern

Close the service valve on the 15 mm cold supply pipe entering the cistern, or close the mains stopcock if there is no valve. Lift the float arm to keep the valve shut while the cistern drains, or tie it up with a length of string to a batten across the top of the tank. Open cold taps below to drain the cistern down to the level of the valve inlet.

Unscrew the float arm from the valve body. Hold the valve body with the water pump pliers to stop it rotating in the cistern wall and unscrew the back nut from the outside of the tank with the adjustable spanner. Withdraw the valve body through the hole in the cistern wall.
Step 3, fit the new valve and set the float level

Wrap PTFE tape clockwise onto the threaded inlet of the new valve body. Insert it through the cistern wall hole from the inside and tighten the back nut firmly on the outside. Attach the float arm and set the float position so the water level rises to 25 mm below the overflow pipe before the valve closes. Restore the supply and watch the cistern fill; adjust the float arm bend or adjustment nut until the shut-off level is correct.

Setting the float too high. If the float is set so the cistern fills to within 50 mm of the overflow, any increase in mains pressure at night causes the overflow to run. Set the shut-off level 25 mm below the overflow outlet.
Fitting the wrong inlet size. Older cisterns may have a 22 mm inlet; measure the existing valve inlet before buying the replacement, not just the supply pipe diameter.
